original_abstract: The three-dimensional architecture of carbohelicenes has long captivated molecular chemists, leading to their integration into a diverse array of functional applications. In particular, their intense circularly polarized luminescence has sparked significant interest for the development of next-generation optical materials. While enantioselective routes to fused carbo- and heterohelicenes are well-established, a direct catalytic method for synthesizing lower, non-fused carbo[*n*]helicenes (*n* = 4–6) has remained elusive. Here, we describe a general and efficient synthesis of these lower carbo[*n*]helicenes via palladium-catalyzed enantioselective C–H arylation, enabled by a specialized bifunctional phosphine-carboxylate ligand. Computational modeling suggests that enantioselectivity is governed by a cooperative effect between the C–H activation and reductive elimination stages. This enantio-induction stems from a sophisticated interplay of non-covalent interactions and steric repulsion between the substrate and the ligand during two pivotal reductive elimination steps. Finally, we provide a systematic characterization of the photophysical and chiroptical properties of the resulting scalemic [*n*]helicenes.
humanized_abstract: The structural features of carbohelicenes have made them subjects of considerable research. Nowadays they are increasingly being used as components in the preparation of various materials with useful functions. In particular, their strong CPD signals attracted much interest for the design of novel light materials. While various methods, both enantioselective and not, have been reported for the preparation of enantiomeric fused carbo- and heterohelicenes, to the best of our knowledge, no catalyst-assisted method is reported for the preparation of the lower non-fused carbo[*n*]helicenes of interest for numerous applications and structural studies. Here we show an efficient general synthesis of these compounds by means of palladium-catalysed enantioselective C–H activation reaction employing a newly synthesised bifunctional bifluorophosphate–phosphine–carboxylate-ligand and theoretical insights reveal that the enantioselection is the result of the cooperation between C–H activation and the following reductive elimination. These non-covalent effects are sustained by severe steric repulsions operating between the catalyst–ligand complex and the molecular transition state of the less stable reductive elimination in each of the two successive cycles. The photophysical and chiroptical properties of the produced enantiomeric mixtures (scalemic) [*n*]helicenes have been carefully and systematically investigated.
